summary:
Intravascular fluids fit well into standard classifications. Interstitium and extracellular spaces align closely with recognized physiological models but peritoneum represents an organ-specific space rather than a broad category of bodily fluid distribution or exchange mechanisms.
The peritoneal cavity contains tissue that surrounds abdominal organs; thus, it doesn't represent one of the main categories used to describe different types of body fluids like interstitial or intravascular components do.
To summarize:

Intravascular refers specifically to contents within blood vessels fitting typical categorizations while Peritoneal pertains uniquely as visceral encapsulation without being part of broader systemic fluid dynamics classification systems such as those involving interstitial vs intra/extra cellular distinctions which better define major divisions amongst various biological liquids throughout organisms' bodies including humans. Agent1 concludes by stating clearly why "Peritoneal" isn’t considered under common general body fluid compartments—because its primary function relates more narrowly around containing tissues versus participating broadly in overall liquid exchanges seen through other terms listed. This helps pinpoint exactly what makes each option either suitable or unsuitable based strictly upon conventional medical understanding regarding human anatomy/systems organization focusing particularly where specific regions might stand outside usual scope when discussing larger scale functional roles played via transport networks etc.. 

Key Points Summarized In Two Sentences For Clarity And Precision :
- Intravascular matches traditional fluid division criteria
- Peritoneal defines localized structural boundary instead serving specialized role unrelated to mainstream circulation/distribution patterns defining core body fluid classes .
